enrichment.short_description: Apiary was an API design, mock server, and documentation platform built around the open-source API Blueprint format. It was acquired by Oracle in 2017 and is now being wound down, with Oracle shutting the service off in September 2026.
enrichment.headquarters: San Francisco, California, USA (originally); now operated as part of Oracle Corporation
enrichment.seo_title: Apiary.io Review 2026: History, Features and Oracle Shutdown
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Apiary.io was an API design and documentation tool acquired by Oracle in 2017. Learn what it offered, its current status, and the 2026 shutdown timeline.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Apiary was an API design, mocking, and documentation platform founded in San Francisco in 2011 by Jan Moravec and Jakub Nesetril. It became known for creating and championing API Blueprint, an open-source, Markdown-based specification format for describing REST APIs, which it used to power live documentation, interactive mock servers, and automated testing directly from an API description. Before its acquisition, Apiary raised more than 8 million dollars in funding, including a 6.8 million dollar Series A in 2015, backed by investors such as Flybridge, Baseline Ventures, and Credo Ventures, and grew to support more than 160,000 to 590,000 APIs across roughly 486,000 registered users at its peak. Oracle acquired Apiary in January 2017 to bolster its API management and cloud integration portfolio, folding Apiary's design-and-mock capabilities into Oracle's broader API Platform Cloud Service and, later, Oracle Cloud Infrastructure. Paid Apiary subscription plans were deprecated back in 2018, meaning the product has effectively run as a free legacy tool for existing users for several years rather than an actively sold commercial product. As of 2026, Oracle has formally announced that Apiary is being retired, with a planned shutdown around September 2026, and Oracle is actively directing remaining users to migrate their API documentation, mock servers, and Blueprint files into other Oracle Cloud tooling before that date. In practical terms, Apiary is not a viable choice for anyone starting a new API documentation project today. It has had no meaningful new feature development in years, no modern pricing plans, and no long-term future as a standalone brand. Teams still using it for legacy documentation should treat this as an active migration deadline, moving specs to OpenAPI/Swagger-based tools such as SwaggerHub, Stoplight, Redocly, Postman, or Bump.sh, all of which offer comparable or superior design, mocking, and documentation capabilities with active support. For historical and research purposes, Apiary is notable as one of the earlier API-design-first platforms and as the origin of API Blueprint, which influenced how a generation of API tooling approached documentation-as-code, even though the OpenAPI/Swagger format has since become the dominant industry standard instead of API Blueprint.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is apiary.io
paragraphs: Apiary was one of the earliest dedicated API design and documentation platforms, founded in San Francisco in 2011. Its core idea was 'API design first': teams would write a human-readable specification using Apiary's own open-source API Blueprint format, and Apiary would automatically generate interactive documentation, mock servers, and test suites from that single source of truth.

Oracle acquired Apiary in January 2017 to strengthen its API management and cloud offerings, and the product was folded into Oracle's API Platform Cloud Service and later Oracle Cloud Infrastructure. Paid plans were phased out in 2018, and Oracle has since confirmed it is retiring the service entirely, with a shutdown expected around September 2026.

Anyone evaluating apiary.io today should treat it as a legacy, end-of-life product rather than an active purchasing option. Existing users are being pointed toward migrating their specifications and documentation into other Oracle Cloud tools or third-party alternatives ahead of the shutdown date.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features (Historical)
paragraphs: At its peak, Apiary offered an editor for writing API Blueprint specifications, live-updating interactive documentation generated directly from that spec, and a mock server that let front-end and back-end teams work in parallel before real endpoints existed.

It also included automated testing (dredd, an open-source tool originating from Apiary, validated real API responses against the Blueprint spec), team collaboration on shared specs, and integrations for embedding documentation into a company's own developer portal.

These capabilities were considered ahead of their time in 2013 to 2015, but the broader API tooling market has since standardized around the OpenAPI (Swagger) specification rather than Apiary's own API Blueprint format, which reduced Apiary's relative advantage over time.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Apiary no longer has an active commercial pricing structure. Paid subscription tiers were deprecated in 2018, after which the tool was accessible for free to existing users under Oracle's stewardship.

There is no current signup flow for new paid customers, and with Oracle's confirmed shutdown of the service planned for September 2026, prospective users should not budget for or plan around apiary.io as an ongoing paid product.

Teams still relying on Apiary for documentation should plan a migration budget for a replacement platform (such as SwaggerHub, Stoplight, Redocly, or Postman) rather than expecting continued free or paid access.

features.title: API Blueprint specification
features.description: A Markdown-based, human-readable format for describing REST APIs that Apiary created and championed as an alternative to OpenAPI/Swagger.

features.title: Interactive documentation
features.description: Automatically generated, browsable API documentation rendered directly from the Blueprint specification.

features.title: Mock servers
features.description: Instant mock endpoints generated from the spec, allowing front-end and back-end teams to build in parallel before real APIs existed.

features.title: Automated contract testing
features.description: Validation tooling (via the dredd project, which originated at Apiary) that checked live API responses against the documented spec.

features.title: Team collaboration
features.description: Shared workspaces for teams to co-author and review API specifications.

features.title: Oracle Cloud integration
features.description: After acquisition, Apiary specs could be linked into Oracle API Platform Cloud Service for governance and management.

pros: Historically one of the first true design-first API platforms, well ahead of much of the market in 2013 to 2015
Created API Blueprint and the dredd testing tool, both influential in the early API tooling ecosystem
Backed by Oracle's infrastructure after acquisition, giving it enterprise-grade reliability while it operated
Free to use for existing legacy documentation for years after paid plans were dropped
cons: Being fully shut down by Oracle, with a planned retirement around September 2026
No active new feature development or investment for years
Built around API Blueprint rather than the now-dominant OpenAPI/Swagger standard, limiting compatibility with modern tooling
No current paid plans or new-customer signup path
Existing users must migrate documentation elsewhere before shutdown, adding urgency and switching cost

faqs.question: Is Apiary.io still available in 2026?
faqs.answer: It is still technically online, but Oracle has announced it is being retired, with a shutdown planned around September 2026. It should be treated as end-of-life.

faqs.question: Who owns Apiary now?
faqs.answer: Oracle Corporation, which acquired Apiary in January 2017.

faqs.question: Can I sign up for a new Apiary account?
faqs.answer: There is no meaningful active commercial onboarding for new customers; the product is in wind-down mode.

faqs.question: What should current Apiary users do?
faqs.answer: Migrate API specifications and documentation to another platform, such as SwaggerHub, Stoplight, Redocly, Postman, or Bump.sh, before the announced shutdown.

faqs.question: What was API Blueprint?
faqs.answer: API Blueprint was Apiary's own open-source, Markdown-based specification language for describing REST APIs, an alternative to OpenAPI/Swagger.

faqs.question: Did Apiary ever have paid plans?
faqs.answer: Yes, historically it offered paid subscription tiers, but these were deprecated in 2018 and the tool has run as a free legacy service since.

faqs.question: Is Apiary the same as Oracle API Platform Cloud Service?
faqs.answer: No, but Apiary's design and documentation capabilities were integrated into Oracle's broader API management offerings after the 2017 acquisition.

use_cases.title: Migrating legacy documentation
use_cases.description: Teams that still have API specs stored in Apiary need to export and migrate their API Blueprint files to a modern platform before the shutdown.
use_cases.audience: Existing Apiary users and API teams

use_cases.title: Historical API design research
use_cases.description: Researchers and API tooling builders studying the evolution of design-first API development and the origins of API Blueprint and contract testing.
use_cases.audience: API tooling researchers and developer advocates

use_cases.title: Evaluating OpenAPI-based replacements
use_cases.description: Organizations comparing modern API documentation and mock server platforms as a direct Apiary replacement.
use_cases.audience: Engineering and platform teams choosing new API tooling

enrichment.short_description: ApiFlash is a website screenshot API built on headless Chrome and AWS Lambda that lets developers capture full-page, mobile, and custom-viewport screenshots of any URL through a single REST endpoint.
enrichment.seo_title: ApiFlash Review 2026: Website Screenshot API Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: ApiFlash is a Chrome and AWS Lambda based screenshot API. See 2026 pricing tiers, features, and how it compares for developers capturing web screenshots.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: ApiFlash is a website screenshot API that lets developers convert any URL into a PNG, JPEG, or WebP image through a single REST call. It is built on top of Google Chrome running in headless mode and AWS Lambda, which the company positions as giving it fast, scalable, and consistently up-to-date rendering compared to self-hosted browser automation. ApiFlash was founded in 2018 by Timothee Jeannin and is operated by NetCube EURL, a small technology company based in Montpellier, France that has run the service since 2017; the company has remained self-funded and bootstrapped, reportedly operating with a single-person team as of recent revenue data, while serving a claimed 12,000 to 13,000-plus businesses capturing millions of screenshots per day. Its feature set covers full-page and mobile-viewport screenshots, custom viewport dimensions, automatic detection of when a page has finished loading before capturing, ad and cookie-banner blocking for cleaner captures, CSS injection to hide or modify page elements before rendering, geolocation targeting, and direct export to Amazon S3. Pricing follows a straightforward tiered-subscription model based on monthly screenshot volume: a free plan offers 100 screenshots per month with no credit card required, followed by paid tiers priced at 7 dollars for 1,000 screenshots, 35 dollars for 10,000, and 180 dollars for 100,000, with a custom Enterprise tier for businesses needing millions of screenshots per month with top-priority support. ApiFlash's target audience is developers and small-to-mid-sized product teams who need reliable programmatic screenshots for use cases such as social sharing preview images, visual monitoring of competitor or client websites, automated QA and visual regression testing, PDF or thumbnail generation pipelines, and no-code/low-code automation tools like Zapier or Make. Compared to alternatives such as Urlbox, ScreenshotOne, Microlink, and browserless.io, ApiFlash is generally positioned as a simple, no-frills, single-endpoint option that emphasizes speed (sub-one-second response times for most requests) and ease of integration over an extensive feature catalog, making it a good fit for teams that want screenshot capture to be a small, reliable utility rather than a complex platform to manage.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is ApiFlash
paragraphs: ApiFlash is a screenshot-as-a-service API that converts any web page URL into an image file through a simple HTTP request. It runs headless Chrome instances on AWS Lambda infrastructure, which the company says lets it scale to millions of screenshots per day while keeping response times under a second for most requests.

Founded in 2018 and run by a small French team (NetCube EURL, based in Montpellier), ApiFlash has built a long-running, profitable niche business serving thousands of developers and companies who need programmatic screenshots without maintaining their own browser automation infrastructure.
The product deliberately stays narrow in scope: rather than trying to be a full browser automation platform, it focuses specifically on being a fast, reliable, single-purpose screenshot endpoint.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: ApiFlash supports full-page and mobile/responsive screenshots, custom viewport widths and heights, and three output formats: PNG, JPEG, and WebP. It automatically detects when a page has finished loading (including JavaScript-heavy pages) before capturing, which helps avoid blank or partially rendered screenshots.

Additional options include ad-blocking and cookie-banner removal for cleaner captures, custom CSS injection to hide or restyle elements before the screenshot is taken, IP geolocation targeting to render region-specific content, and element-specific capture for grabbing just a portion of a page.

Captured images can be returned directly or exported to Amazon S3 for storage, and the service is used by companies such as Canonical, Accenture, Drift, Thomson Reuters, and ManyChat, according to ApiFlash's published customer list.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: ApiFlash uses a tiered subscription model priced by monthly screenshot volume. The Free plan includes 100 screenshots per month at no cost and requires no credit card.
Paid plans start at the Lite tier for 7 dollars per month (1,000 screenshots), scaling to Medium at 35 dollars per month (10,000 screenshots) and Large at 180 dollars per month (100,000 screenshots).
Businesses needing millions of screenshots per month can request custom Enterprise pricing with top-priority support and dedicated SLAs.

features.title: Headless Chrome rendering
features.description: Screenshots are captured using up-to-date Chrome instances for accurate, modern web rendering.

features.title: Full-page and mobile capture
features.description: Supports both full-scrolling-page screenshots and mobile/responsive viewport rendering.

features.title: Automatic load detection
features.description: Waits for pages, including JavaScript-heavy ones, to finish loading before taking the screenshot.

features.title: Multiple output formats
features.description: Returns images as PNG, JPEG, or WebP depending on the request.

features.title: Ad and cookie-banner blocking
features.description: Removes ads and cookie consent overlays for cleaner captures.

features.title: CSS injection
features.description: Lets developers hide or restyle page elements before the screenshot is taken.

features.title: S3 export
features.description: Screenshots can be exported directly to an Amazon S3 bucket.

features.title: AWS Lambda scalability
features.description: Runs on serverless AWS Lambda infrastructure for fast, scalable throughput.

pros: Very fast response times, typically under one second per screenshot
Simple, single-endpoint API that is easy to integrate quickly
Generous, no-credit-card-required free tier for testing
Long track record (operating since 2017 to 2018) and a large existing customer base
Useful extras like ad blocking, cookie-banner removal, and S3 export
cons: Run by a very small (reportedly one-person) team, which may limit support responsiveness at scale
Narrower feature set than full browser automation platforms like Apify or Browserless
Entry paid tier (7 dollars for 1,000 screenshots) is a relatively small volume compared to some competitors' entry tiers
No visible mention of advanced actions like scrolling scripts, multi-step interactions, or PDF generation

faqs.question: What does ApiFlash do?
faqs.answer: It is a REST API that takes a URL and returns a screenshot image of that web page, rendered using headless Chrome.

faqs.question: Does ApiFlash have a free plan?
faqs.answer: Yes, it offers 100 free screenshots per month with no credit card required.

faqs.question: What image formats does ApiFlash support?
faqs.answer: PNG, JPEG, and WebP.

faqs.question: How much does ApiFlash cost?
faqs.answer: Paid plans start at 7 dollars per month for 1,000 screenshots, scaling up to 180 dollars per month for 100,000 screenshots, with custom Enterprise pricing above that.

faqs.question: Who founded ApiFlash?
faqs.answer: It was founded in 2018 by Timothee Jeannin and is operated by NetCube EURL, based in Montpellier, France.

faqs.question: Can ApiFlash capture full-page and mobile screenshots?
faqs.answer: Yes, it supports full-page scrolling captures as well as custom and mobile viewport sizes.

faqs.question: Does ApiFlash store screenshots?
faqs.answer: It can export captured screenshots directly to an Amazon S3 bucket if configured.

use_cases.title: Social sharing preview images
use_cases.description: Automatically generate link preview or open graph images from live web pages.

use_cases.title: Website monitoring and QA
use_cases.description: Capture periodic screenshots of pages to visually monitor changes or run visual regression checks.

use_cases.title: No-code automation workflows
use_cases.description: Trigger screenshot capture inside Zapier, Make, or similar automation tools as part of a broader workflow.
use_cases.audience: No-code builders and small business owners

enrichment.short_description: Apify is a Prague-based web scraping and browser automation platform where developers build, run, and share cloud programs called Actors, powering a public marketplace of tens of thousands of ready-made scrapers and AI agents.
enrichment.seo_title: Apify Review 2026: Web Scraping Platform Pricing and Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Apify is a Prague-based web scraping and browser automation platform with an Actor marketplace. See 2026 pricing, features, and use cases for developers.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Apify is a cloud platform for web scraping, data extraction, and browser automation, founded in 2015 in Prague, Czech Republic, by Jan Curn and Jakub Balada, who met studying computer science and built the company partly through a Y Combinator Fellowship in Mountain View. The company has been largely bootstrapped, raising only around 0.5 million dollars in early seed funding before becoming self-sustaining, then raising a further roughly 2.98 million dollar seed round in April 2024 led by J&T Ventures with participation from Reflex Capital, bringing total disclosed funding to about 3.5 million dollars; independent estimates put its annual recurring revenue in the tens of millions of dollars. Apify's core concept is the 'Actor': a serverless cloud program, typically a web scraper, crawler, or automation script, that runs on Apify's infrastructure and can be published to the public Apify Store, a marketplace with tens of thousands of ready-made Actors covering targets like Google Search, Amazon, Instagram, LinkedIn, TikTok, Google Maps, and general-purpose website crawlers. Developers can either use pre-built Actors immediately, pay per result or per usage to rent Actors built by others (with revenue shared back to Actor developers), or build and deploy their own Actors using the Apify SDK and Crawlee, Apify's open-source scraping and browser automation library that supports Puppeteer, Playwright, and Cheerio-based extraction. The platform provides supporting infrastructure including a residential and datacenter proxy network, scheduled and API/webhook-triggered runs, structured data storage (datasets, key-value stores, request queues), and integrations with tools like Zapier, Make, n8n, and LangChain, positioning it as increasingly relevant for feeding structured web data into AI agents and LLM pipelines. Pricing is usage-based on top of a subscription: a Free plan includes 5 dollars of monthly platform credit with limited concurrency and proxy access; Starter is 29 dollars per month (26 dollars per month billed annually); Scale is 199 dollars per month; and Business is 999 dollars per month, each tier raising concurrent run limits, RAM ceilings, proxy discounts, and support levels, with custom Enterprise pricing above that. Apify's target users range from individual developers and no-code automation builders to data teams and AI companies that need reliable, maintained web data pipelines without building and hosting their own scraping infrastructure, and it competes most directly with platforms like Bright Data, ScraperAPI, Zyte, and Octoparse.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Apify
paragraphs: Apify is a cloud platform for web scraping, data extraction, and browser automation. Founded in 2015 in Prague, Czech Republic, by Jan Curn and Jakub Balada, the company built its business around the idea of turning any website into a structured API in minutes.

The platform's central unit is the 'Actor,' a serverless program that runs on Apify's cloud infrastructure. Actors can scrape websites, automate browser tasks, or process data, and they can be published to the Apify Store, a public marketplace with tens of thousands of ready-made Actors that other users can run on demand.

Apify has been largely bootstrapped, with only about 3.5 million dollars in total disclosed funding across a small seed round and a 2024 follow-on seed led by J&T Ventures, and it reportedly generates tens of millions of dollars in annual recurring revenue.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The Apify Store gives users instant access to pre-built scrapers for popular targets such as Google Search, Amazon, Google Maps, Instagram, LinkedIn, and TikTok, so many use cases don't require writing any scraping code at all.

For custom needs, developers can build their own Actors using the Apify SDK and Crawlee, Apify's open-source library supporting Puppeteer, Playwright, and lightweight Cheerio-based extraction, then deploy them to Apify's serverless cloud with automatic scaling.

The platform bundles supporting infrastructure that most scraping projects need anyway: a residential and datacenter proxy network, scheduling and webhook triggers, structured storage for scraped data, and integrations with Zapier, Make, n8n, and LangChain for feeding data into automation workflows and AI agents.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Apify uses a subscription-plus-usage model. The Free plan includes 5 dollars of monthly platform credit along with limited concurrency, proxy access, and community support, enough for light testing and small projects.

Paid plans scale from Starter at 29 dollars per month (26 dollars per month if billed annually) up through Scale at 199 dollars per month and Business at 999 dollars per month, each raising concurrent run limits, maximum RAM per run, proxy pricing discounts, and support tier (chat support, then priority chat support, then a dedicated account manager).

Actual monthly cost depends heavily on compute usage (billed per compute unit) and proxy bandwidth (billed per gigabyte, with rates improving at higher tiers), so most teams' real spend is usage-driven on top of the base subscription.

features.title: Apify Store marketplace
features.description: A public marketplace of tens of thousands of ready-made Actors (scrapers and automation tools) for popular websites and use cases.

features.title: Custom Actor development
features.description: Build and deploy custom scraping or automation programs using the Apify SDK and the open-source Crawlee library.

features.title: Headless browser automation
features.description: Native support for Puppeteer, Playwright, and Selenium-based browser automation at scale.

features.title: Built-in proxy network
features.description: Residential and datacenter proxies included and billed per gigabyte to help avoid blocking during scraping.

features.title: Scheduling and webhooks
features.description: Run Actors on a schedule or trigger them via API calls and webhooks for automated pipelines.

features.title: Structured data storage
features.description: Built-in datasets, key-value stores, and request queues for managing scraped data and crawl state.

features.title: Automation and AI integrations
features.description: Connects to Zapier, Make, n8n, and LangChain, positioning scraped data for use in automation and LLM workflows.

features.title: Actor monetization
features.description: Developers can publish and monetize their own Actors in the Apify Store, earning revenue when others rent them.

pros: Massive library of ready-made scrapers in the Apify Store, reducing the need to build from scratch
Flexible enough for both no-code users and developers who want full custom control
Built-in proxy network and infrastructure removes a major operational burden of large-scale scraping
Strong fit for feeding structured web data into AI agents and LLM pipelines
Long operating history (since 2015) with a large, active developer community
cons: Usage-based compute and proxy costs can add up quickly for large-scale or continuous scraping jobs
Learning curve for building custom Actors compared to simpler single-purpose scraping APIs
Quality and maintenance of third-party Actors in the Store varies since it's a marketplace
Free tier's 5-dollar monthly credit is quite limited for anything beyond light testing

faqs.question: What is an Apify Actor?
faqs.answer: An Actor is a serverless cloud program on Apify, typically a web scraper, crawler, or browser automation script, that can be run on demand, scheduled, or published to the Apify Store.

faqs.question: Do I need to know how to code to use Apify?
faqs.answer: No. You can rent and run pre-built Actors from the Apify Store without writing code, though building custom Actors requires JavaScript, TypeScript, or Python.

faqs.question: How much does Apify cost?
faqs.answer: There is a free plan with 5 dollars of monthly platform credit; paid plans start at 29 dollars per month (Starter) and scale to 199 dollars (Scale) and 999 dollars (Business), plus usage-based compute and proxy charges.

faqs.question: Who founded Apify?
faqs.answer: Apify was founded in 2015 in Prague, Czech Republic, by Jan Curn and Jakub Balada.

faqs.question: Does Apify provide proxies?
faqs.answer: Yes, it includes access to a residential and datacenter proxy network, billed per gigabyte, with better rates on higher-tier plans.

faqs.question: Can I make money on Apify?
faqs.answer: Yes, developers can publish Actors to the Apify Store and earn revenue when other users rent or run them.

faqs.question: What is Crawlee?
faqs.answer: Crawlee is Apify's open-source scraping and browser automation library, used to build Actors with support for Puppeteer, Playwright, and Cheerio.

faqs.question: How does Apify compare to Bright Data or ScraperAPI?
faqs.answer: Apify differentiates itself with its Actor marketplace and full automation platform, while Bright Data and ScraperAPI focus more narrowly on proxy and scraping infrastructure rather than a public marketplace of pre-built scrapers.

use_cases.title: E-commerce and price monitoring
use_cases.description: Scrape product listings, prices, and reviews from marketplaces like Amazon for competitive intelligence.

use_cases.title: Lead generation and social data extraction
use_cases.description: Extract structured data from LinkedIn, Instagram, or Google Maps for sales and marketing lead lists.

use_cases.title: AI agent and LLM data pipelines
use_cases.description: Feed real-time structured web data into AI agents, RAG pipelines, or LLM applications via Apify integrations.

enrichment.short_description: APITemplate.io is an API that generates PDFs and images, such as certificates, invoices, reports, and social media graphics, from reusable templates built in a WYSIWYG or HTML/CSS editor.
enrichment.seo_title: APITemplate.io Review 2026: PDF and Image API Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: APITemplate.io generates PDFs and images from reusable templates via API. See 2026 pricing plans, features, and use cases for developers and marketers.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: APITemplate.io is a document and image generation API founded in 2020 by Jacky Tan and based in Singapore. It lets developers and no-code users create reusable templates, using either a WYSIWYG drag-and-drop editor or raw HTML and CSS, and then generate finished PDF documents or images programmatically by merging dynamic JSON data into those templates. Common outputs include invoices, contracts, certificates, reports, receipts, and social media graphics such as Instagram or Facebook post images, product cards, and quote graphics. The platform supports both synchronous generation (returning the file immediately in the API response) and asynchronous generation with webhook callbacks for larger batch jobs, and it ships official SDKs for Python, C#, Java, PHP, and JavaScript, plus native integrations with Zapier, Make, and n8n for no-code automation workflows. Pricing is structured around two tracks: PDF-only plans and combined PDF-plus-image plans. On the PDF-only track, a free plan offers 50 PDFs and 3 templates per month at no cost, a PDF Basic plan is 19 dollars per month for 3,000 PDFs and 20 templates with CDN storage, custom CSS, and async webhooks, and a PDF Standard plan is 69 dollars per month for 12,000 PDFs, 180 templates, and team collaboration features. On the combined track, a Starter plan is 29 dollars per month for 1,500 PDFs or images and 15 templates, a Standard plan is 69 dollars per month for 9,000 PDFs or images and 150 templates with team collaboration, and an Enterprise plan is 139 dollars per month for 20,000 PDFs or images, unlimited templates, AWS S3 integration, and priority support; monthly billing (versus annual) carries roughly a 15 to 29 percent price premium. APITemplate.io's target audience is developers, SaaS founders, and marketing or operations teams who need to automate document or image generation inside a larger product or workflow, such as generating a certificate every time a course is completed, an invoice every time an order is placed, or a branded social graphic every time a blog post is published, without building a custom rendering pipeline from scratch. It competes with tools like PDF Generator API, CraftMyPDF, Templated.io, and DocRaptor, generally differentiating on its template editor usability and combined PDF-plus-image feature set.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is APITemplate.io
paragraphs: APITemplate.io is an API-first platform for generating PDF documents and images from reusable templates. Instead of writing custom rendering code, users design a template once, in either a visual WYSIWYG editor or raw HTML and CSS, and then call an API endpoint with dynamic JSON data to produce a finished file on demand.

Founded in 2020 by Jacky Tan and based in Singapore, the platform is built for developers and product teams who need to automate document or image creation as part of a larger application, such as generating invoices, certificates, contracts, reports, or social media graphics automatically.

It supports both instant, synchronous file generation and asynchronous batch generation with webhook callbacks, making it suitable for both real-time, single-document requests and large bulk-generation jobs.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Templates can be built visually with a drag-and-drop WYSIWYG editor, or coded directly in HTML and CSS for full design control, with a real-time preview so designers can see changes before publishing a template.
Dynamic data merging lets templates pull in variables such as customer names, order totals, or dates from a JSON payload, so the same template can produce thousands of unique documents or images.

The platform includes official SDKs for Python, C#, Java, PHP, and JavaScript, plus integrations with Zapier, Make, and n8n, and higher-tier plans add CDN-backed file storage, custom CSS, async webhook delivery, team collaboration, and AWS S3 export.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: APITemplate.io offers a free plan with 50 PDFs and 3 templates per month, useful for testing and very small-scale use.

Paid plans split into a PDF-only track (PDF Basic at 19 dollars per month for 3,000 PDFs and 20 templates, PDF Standard at 69 dollars per month for 12,000 PDFs and 180 templates) and a combined PDF-plus-image track (Starter at 29 dollars per month for 1,500 documents, Standard at 69 dollars per month for 9,000 documents, and Enterprise at 139 dollars per month for 20,000 documents with unlimited templates and AWS S3 integration).
Monthly billing carries a premium of roughly 15 to 29 percent over annual billing, so teams with predictable volume can save meaningfully by committing annually.

features.title: WYSIWYG template editor
features.description: Visual drag-and-drop editor for designing PDF and image templates without writing code.

features.title: HTML and CSS templates
features.description: Full custom control over template design using raw HTML and CSS for advanced layouts.

features.title: Dynamic data merging
features.description: Populate templates with dynamic values from a JSON payload to generate unique documents per request.

features.title: Synchronous and asynchronous generation
features.description: Get files back instantly in the API response, or generate large batches asynchronously with webhook callbacks.

features.title: Image generation for social graphics
features.description: Create branded social media images, product cards, and marketing graphics programmatically.

features.title: Multi-language SDKs
features.description: Official SDKs for Python, C#, Java, PHP, and JavaScript for faster integration.

features.title: No-code integrations
features.description: Native connections to Zapier, Make, and n8n for automation without custom backend code.

features.title: Cloud storage export
features.description: CDN storage and AWS S3 integration on higher-tier plans for hosting generated files.

pros: Supports both PDFs and images from a single platform, covering more use cases than PDF-only tools
Flexible template creation via either visual editor or raw HTML/CSS for full design control
Reasonable free tier for testing before committing to a paid plan
Broad SDK and no-code integration support (Zapier, Make, n8n)
Asynchronous generation with webhooks supports large-scale batch document creation
cons: Pricing structure split across separate PDF-only and combined tracks can be confusing to compare
Monthly billing carries a meaningful premium over annual plans
Template limits on lower tiers (as few as 3 to 20 templates) may be restrictive for larger catalogs
Enterprise-grade features like AWS S3 integration and unlimited templates require the top-tier plan

faqs.question: What can I generate with APITemplate.io?
faqs.answer: PDF documents such as invoices, certificates, contracts, and reports, as well as images such as social media graphics and product cards, all from reusable templates.

faqs.question: Do I need to know how to code?
faqs.answer: No, templates can be built with a visual WYSIWYG editor, though HTML and CSS are also supported for advanced customization.

faqs.question: What does APITemplate.io cost?
faqs.answer: A free plan offers 50 PDFs per month; paid plans start at 19 dollars per month for PDF-only usage or 29 dollars per month for combined PDF and image generation.

faqs.question: Can APITemplate.io generate files asynchronously?
faqs.answer: Yes, it supports asynchronous generation with webhook callbacks, useful for large batch jobs.

faqs.question: Which programming languages have official SDKs?
faqs.answer: Python, C#, Java, PHP, and JavaScript.

faqs.question: Does it integrate with no-code tools?
faqs.answer: Yes, it integrates with Zapier, Make, and n8n.

faqs.question: Who founded APITemplate.io?
faqs.answer: It was founded in 2020 by Jacky Tan and is based in Singapore.

enrichment.short_description: APIVerve is a single subscription that unlocks 300 plus utility, data, and AI-powered REST APIs, such as email validation, geocoding, and text analysis, through one API key and a shared credit-based billing pool.
enrichment.seo_title: APIVerve Review 2026: 300+ API Bundle Pricing Compared
enrichment.seo_meta_description: APIVerve bundles 300 plus utility and data APIs under one key with credit-based pricing. See 2026 plans, features, and how it compares to alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: APIVerve is a developer platform that bundles more than 300 individual REST APIs, covering utility functions, real-time data, AI and machine learning tasks, and location services, behind a single API key and a single shared credit-based billing pool, rather than requiring a separate subscription for each API. The catalog spans categories such as email and phone validation, IP geolocation, currency conversion, text and sentiment analysis, image processing, QR code generation, and similar small utility endpoints, positioning it as a direct alternative to bundle-style API marketplaces like Abstract API and APILayer. The public GitHub organization lists 356-plus production-ready APIs in the catalog. Billing works on a credit system: every account gets a recurring pool of free or paid credits each month, and each individual API call consumes a fixed number of credits, typically 1 to 5 depending on the complexity of that specific API, rather than being billed per-API. The free plan provides a limited number of credits per month (100 credits according to the current pricing page) with a 5 requests-per-minute rate limit, community support, and non-commercial use only, making it suitable for testing and prototyping but not for production or commercial deployment. Paid tiers scale from a Hobby plan at 12.99 dollars per month (15,000 credits) up through Starter at 29.99 dollars per month (100,000 credits, commercial license, and no-code integrations with Zapier, Make, and MCP), Pro at 99.99 dollars per month (500,000 credits, team seats, scoped sub-keys, and full GraphQL access), and Mega at 299.99 dollars per month (2,000,000 credits, more team seats, dedicated servers, and extended audit history), with custom Enterprise pricing above that; all plans are billed monthly only, with no annual discount option. The company behind the product is listed as EvlarSoft LLC in the site's footer. APIVerve's target audience is developers and small teams who want to avoid juggling dozens of separate API vendor subscriptions for common utility tasks, trading the ability to pick the single best-in-class provider for each function in exchange for consolidated billing, a single integration pattern, and one dashboard to manage rate limits, usage, and access across the entire catalog.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is APIVerve
paragraphs: APIVerve is a bundled API marketplace that gives developers access to more than 300 individual utility, data, and AI-powered REST APIs through a single API key, rather than requiring separate signups and subscriptions for each one.

The catalog covers common developer needs such as email and phone number validation, IP geolocation, currency conversion, text and sentiment analysis, image processing, and QR code generation, functioning similarly to bundle-style competitors like Abstract API and APILayer.

Rather than pricing each API separately, APIVerve uses a shared credit pool: a fixed monthly credit allowance is consumed at a variable rate (typically 1 to 5 credits per call) depending on which specific API is used.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The core value proposition is consolidation: one API key, one dashboard, and one bill covers access to the entire 300-plus API catalog, removing the overhead of managing dozens of separate vendor relationships for small utility functions.

Higher-tier plans add commercial licensing, higher rate limits, official SDKs, GraphQL access, scoped sub-keys for restricting what different team members or applications can access, and no-code integrations with Zapier, Make, and MCP-based AI agent tooling.
All plans are billed monthly with no long-term contract or annual commitment required, giving teams flexibility to scale usage up or down as needs change.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The Free plan includes 100 credits per month with a 5 requests-per-minute limit, community support, and is restricted to non-commercial use, making it suitable for testing and evaluation only.

Paid plans start at Hobby for 12.99 dollars per month (15,000 credits), followed by Starter at 29.99 dollars per month (100,000 credits, commercial license, and no-code integrations), Pro at 99.99 dollars per month (500,000 credits and team features), and Mega at 299.99 dollars per month (2,000,000 credits and dedicated infrastructure).
Enterprise customers with larger or custom needs can contact APIVerve directly for tailored pricing; all published tiers are billed on a monthly-only basis.

features.title: 300+ API catalog

features.title: Single API key

features.description: Paid tiers unlock commercial use, unlike the non-commercial free plan.

features.title: Scoped sub-keys
features.description: Higher tiers allow creating restricted API sub-keys for team members or specific applications.

features.title: GraphQL access
features.description: Full GraphQL access is available on the Pro plan and above.

features.title: No-code integrations
features.description: Native connections to Zapier, Make, and MCP for use in automation and AI agent workflows.

features.title: Monthly-only billing
features.description: All plans bill monthly with no annual lock-in, offering maximum flexibility.

pros: Consolidates 300 plus APIs under a single key and bill, simplifying vendor management
Transparent, predictable credit-based pricing with no hidden per-API fees
Free tier available for testing and prototyping without a credit card commitment noted as required
No-code integrations (Zapier, Make, MCP) make it accessible beyond pure developers
Flexible monthly billing with no long-term contract
cons: Free plan is restricted to non-commercial use, requiring a paid plan for any production or business application
No annual billing discount, unlike many competitors that reward yearly commitments
Depth of any individual API is likely shallower than a dedicated best-in-class provider for that specific function
Limited public information on the company's founding history, size, and headquarters

faqs.question: What is APIVerve?
faqs.answer: It is a platform that bundles more than 300 individual utility, data, and AI APIs behind a single API key and a shared credit-based billing system.

faqs.question: How does APIVerve pricing work?
faqs.answer: Each account gets a monthly pool of credits, and each API call consumes a fixed number of credits (typically 1 to 5) depending on the specific API used.

faqs.question: Is APIVerve's free plan usable in production?
faqs.answer: No, the free plan is limited to non-commercial use; a paid plan is required for commercial or production applications.

faqs.question: How much does APIVerve cost?
faqs.answer: Paid plans start at 12.99 dollars per month for 15,000 credits, scaling up to 299.99 dollars per month for 2,000,000 credits, with custom Enterprise pricing above that.

faqs.question: Does APIVerve support GraphQL?
faqs.answer: Yes, full GraphQL access is included starting on the Pro plan.

faqs.question: Can APIVerve be used with no-code tools?
faqs.answer: Yes, it integrates with Zapier, Make, and MCP for AI agent and automation workflows.

faqs.question: How many APIs does APIVerve offer?
faqs.answer: More than 300 production-ready APIs, according to the company's own listings and public GitHub catalog.

faqs.question: Who operates APIVerve?
faqs.answer: The service is operated by EvlarSoft LLC, according to the site's footer; detailed public information on founding year and headquarters was not available.

enrichment.short_description: Apollo GraphQL is the company behind Apollo Client, Apollo Server, and the GraphOS platform, widely used tooling for building, federating, and operating GraphQL APIs at scale.
enrichment.seo_title: Apollo GraphQL Review 2026: GraphOS Pricing and Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Apollo GraphQL powers Apollo Client, Apollo Server, and GraphOS. See 2026 pricing tiers, funding history, and how it fits into the GraphQL ecosystem.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Apollo GraphQL is a developer platform company built around GraphQL, the query language and API paradigm that lets client applications request exactly the data they need from one or more backend services. The company traces its roots to 2011, when Geoff Schmidt founded what became Meteor Development Group in San Francisco; the team later pivoted around 2016 to focus on GraphQL tooling and rebranded around the Apollo product line, which has since become one of the most widely adopted toolchains in the GraphQL ecosystem. Apollo has raised roughly 183 million dollars in total funding from investors including Andreessen Horowitz, Insight Partners, Matrix Partners, Trinity Ventures, and MongoDB Ventures, and employs an estimated 180 to 240 people as of 2026. Its product suite includes Apollo Client (open-source GraphQL client libraries for React, iOS, Android, and Kotlin), Apollo Server (an open-source, widely used Node.js GraphQL server), and GraphOS, its commercial platform for managing, observing, and scaling GraphQL APIs in production. GraphOS centers on the concept of the 'supergraph': using Apollo Federation, teams can compose multiple underlying GraphQL or REST services into a single unified graph that client applications query through one endpoint, which GraphOS Studio helps teams design, validate with schema checks in CI, and monitor with usage and performance analytics; the GraphOS Router, a high-performance Rust-based gateway, handles routing and execution at runtime, and Apollo has also added an MCP server capability to make GraphQL APIs consumable by AI agents. Pricing follows a freemium, usage-based model: the Free tier is free forever and includes a self-hosted router limited to 60 requests per minute, schema registry, SSO, and support for up to 3 developers with 1-day data retention; the Developer tier starts at 5 dollars per million requests, billed monthly with no commitment, automatic volume discounts as usage scales (down to roughly 3 dollars per million at very high volumes), a 50-dollar signup credit, support for up to 10 developers, and 7-day retention; the Standard tier is typically priced around 2,500 dollars per month under an annual commitment and supports up to 30 developers with 90-day retention and SLA-backed support; and Enterprise is fully custom, offering unlimited developers, 18-month retention, and 24x7 support with optional professional services. Apollo's target audience spans individual developers and small teams using its free open-source client and server libraries, up through large enterprises running federated, multi-team GraphQL architectures who need GraphOS for governance, observability, and reliability at scale; its main points of comparison in the ecosystem include Hasura, WunderGraph, and cloud-native GraphQL offerings from AWS AppSync and Postgraphile, though Apollo is generally regarded as the de facto standard for enterprise GraphQL federation.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Apollo GraphQL
paragraphs: Apollo GraphQL is the company and product ecosystem behind some of the most widely used GraphQL tooling in the industry, including the open-source Apollo Client and Apollo Server libraries and the commercial GraphOS platform for managing GraphQL APIs in production.

The company's origins trace back to 2011 in San Francisco under founder Geoff Schmidt, initially as Meteor Development Group, before pivoting around 2016 to focus on GraphQL and rebranding around the Apollo name as GraphQL adoption grew across the industry.

Apollo has raised approximately 183 million dollars from investors including Andreessen Horowitz, Insight Partners, and Matrix Partners, and today employs roughly 180 to 240 people, positioning it as one of the largest independent companies focused specifically on the GraphQL ecosystem.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Apollo Client provides open-source GraphQL client libraries for React, iOS, Android, and Kotlin, handling caching, state management, and data fetching for front-end applications. Apollo Server is a popular open-source Node.js server for building GraphQL APIs.

GraphOS, Apollo's commercial platform, is built around the 'supergraph' concept: using Apollo Federation, teams compose multiple backend services into one unified graph that clients query through a single endpoint, with the GraphOS Router (a high-performance Rust-based gateway) handling execution at runtime.

GraphOS Studio provides schema registry, schema checks integrated into CI pipelines, contract variants for exposing different parts of the graph to different consumers, and usage and performance observability, while a newer MCP server capability lets AI agents consume GraphQL APIs directly.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The Free tier is free forever and includes a self-hosted GraphOS router (limited to 60 requests per minute), schema registry, SSO, and support for up to 3 developers with 1-day data retention, suitable for small projects or evaluation.

The Developer tier is usage-based, starting at 5 dollars per million requests with no commitment required, automatic volume discounts as usage grows, and a 50-dollar signup credit, supporting up to 10 developers with 7-day retention.

The Standard tier requires an annual commitment and is typically priced around 2,500 dollars per month for up to 30 developers and 90-day retention, while Enterprise pricing is fully custom, offering unlimited developers, 18-month retention, and 24x7 SLA-backed support.

features.title: Apollo Client
features.description: Open-source GraphQL client libraries for React, iOS, Android, and Kotlin with built-in caching and state management.

features.title: Apollo Server
features.description: A widely used open-source GraphQL server for Node.js.

features.title: Apollo Federation
features.description: Compose multiple GraphQL or REST services into a single unified supergraph queried through one endpoint.

features.title: GraphOS Router
features.description: A high-performance, Rust-based gateway that executes federated GraphQL queries in production.

features.title: Schema registry and checks
features.description: Central schema management with automated checks integrated into CI/CD pipelines to prevent breaking changes.

features.title: GraphOS Studio observability
features.description: Usage analytics, performance monitoring, and error tracking across the entire graph.

features.title: Contract variants
features.description: Expose tailored subsets of the graph schema to different client teams or partners.

features.title: AI-ready MCP server support
features.description: Makes GraphQL APIs consumable by AI agents via the Model Context Protocol.

pros: De facto industry standard for GraphQL federation at enterprise scale
Strong open-source foundation (Apollo Client and Server) with a large, active community
Usage-based Developer tier makes it accessible and affordable for smaller teams to start with
Deep observability and schema governance tooling for large, multi-team GraphQL architectures
Backed by significant funding (about 183 million dollars) and a decade-plus operating history
cons: Standard and Enterprise tiers require custom sales conversations rather than transparent self-serve pricing
Full federation and observability value requires buy-in to the GraphOS platform beyond the free open-source libraries
Cost can scale significantly at high request volumes and larger team sizes
Adds architectural complexity (a supergraph and router layer) that may be overkill for smaller, single-service GraphQL APIs

faqs.question: What is Apollo GraphQL?
faqs.answer: It is the company behind Apollo Client, Apollo Server, and the GraphOS platform, widely used tools for building and operating GraphQL APIs, including federated supergraphs that unify multiple backend services.

faqs.question: Is Apollo GraphQL free to use?
faqs.answer: The Apollo Client and Apollo Server libraries are open source and free, and GraphOS has a free-forever tier; paid GraphOS tiers add higher limits, longer data retention, and enterprise support.

faqs.question: How much does GraphOS cost?
faqs.answer: The Developer tier starts at 5 dollars per million requests with no commitment; Standard is typically priced around 2,500 dollars per month under an annual commitment; Enterprise is custom-priced.

faqs.question: Who founded Apollo GraphQL?
faqs.answer: Its origins trace to Geoff Schmidt's 2011 company Meteor Development Group in San Francisco, which pivoted to focus on GraphQL and became Apollo GraphQL.

faqs.question: What is Apollo Federation?
faqs.answer: A specification and toolset for composing multiple GraphQL or REST services into a single unified 'supergraph' that clients can query through one endpoint.

faqs.question: How much funding has Apollo GraphQL raised?
faqs.answer: Approximately 183 million dollars from investors including Andreessen Horowitz, Insight Partners, and Matrix Partners.

faqs.question: How does Apollo GraphQL compare to Hasura?
faqs.answer: Apollo focuses on federation and unifying existing services into a supergraph, while Hasura focuses more on auto-generating GraphQL APIs directly from databases; many enterprises use Apollo for governance and federation across many services.

faqs.question: Does Apollo GraphQL support AI agent use cases?
faqs.answer: Yes, it has added MCP server support so AI agents can consume GraphQL APIs managed through GraphOS.

enrichment.short_description: App Annie was a pioneering mobile app analytics and market intelligence platform founded in 2010; it rebranded to data.ai around 2021 to 2022, and data.ai was itself acquired by Sensor Tower in March 2024, so the App Annie name now redirects into Sensor Tower's product line.
enrichment.headquarters: San Francisco, California, USA (originally founded in Beijing, China)
enrichment.seo_title: App Annie in 2026: What Happened, data.ai and Sensor Tower
enrichment.seo_meta_description: App Annie rebranded to data.ai, which Sensor Tower acquired in 2024. See the full history, what changed, and where to get mobile app analytics today.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: App Annie was one of the earliest and most prominent mobile app market intelligence platforms, founded in Beijing in 2010 by Bertrand Schmitt and Yann Lechelle, before later moving its headquarters to San Francisco around 2014 to 2015. The company built its business on estimating and selling data about app store downloads, revenue, rankings, and usage across the iOS App Store and Google Play, becoming a widely cited source for mobile industry benchmarking and competitive intelligence, and it raised well over 150 million dollars in venture funding over its history, at one point reaching a valuation in the billions of dollars. In late 2021 to early 2022, App Annie rebranded entirely to data.ai, a name change intended to reflect the company's pivot toward positioning itself as an AI-powered mobile data and analytics company rather than simply an 'app store analytics' brand; this rebrand also coincided with, and was related to, a 2021 SEC settlement in which App Annie and its then-CEO Bertrand Schmitt were charged with securities fraud for manipulating app performance estimates using confidential customer data, resulting in a 10 million dollar penalty. As data.ai, the company continued operating as an independent mobile analytics platform until March 2024, when it was acquired by Sensor Tower, a rival mobile app intelligence firm, in a deal with undisclosed terms; notably, data.ai had raised more than three times the total funding of its acquirer Sensor Tower before the deal closed. Following the acquisition, Sensor Tower absorbed part of data.ai's roughly 400-person team alongside layoffs, and has since worked to combine the two companies' underlying data panels and models into unified products, while for a period keeping both platforms available as nominally standalone offerings. As of 2026, the appannie.com domain redirects to www.data.ai, and the data.ai login page itself now displays a banner stating the company has been acquired by Sensor Tower, directing visitors to Sensor Tower's own site and products for current mobile app analytics services. In short, App Annie no longer exists as an independent, purchasable product or brand: it survives only as a historical name behind data.ai, which is now fully absorbed into Sensor Tower, the company that anyone researching gappsy.com listings should treat as the actual, current provider of this type of mobile app market intelligence data.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Happened To App Annie
paragraphs: App Annie was a mobile app store analytics and market intelligence company founded in Beijing in 2010 by Bertrand Schmitt and Yann Lechelle, later headquartered in San Francisco. For roughly a decade, it was one of the most widely cited sources of app download, revenue, and ranking estimates across iOS and Google Play, used heavily by app publishers, investors, and journalists.

In late 2021 to early 2022, the company fully rebranded from App Annie to data.ai, positioning itself as an AI-driven mobile data company. This followed a 2021 SEC enforcement action in which App Annie and its then-CEO were charged with securities fraud for misusing confidential customer data to alter published app performance estimates, resulting in a 10 million dollar penalty.

As data.ai, the company continued operating independently until March 2024, when it was acquired by Sensor Tower, a competing mobile intelligence firm. As of 2026, the appannie.com and data.ai domains display an acquisition notice and redirect visitors toward Sensor Tower, meaning App Annie no longer exists as an independent, purchasable brand or product.

long_form_blocks.heading: What The Product Offered (Historical)
paragraphs: Under both the App Annie and data.ai names, the platform offered estimates and analytics on app store downloads, revenue, and rankings across the App Store and Google Play, along with usage and engagement data, audience insights, and ad intelligence for competitive benchmarking.

It served publishers, investors, and marketing teams who needed to estimate a competitor's download volume or revenue, track category rankings over time, or benchmark their own app's performance against the broader market.
These core capabilities now live on within Sensor Tower's combined product line, which has worked to merge data.ai's data panels and models with its own since the 2024 acquisition.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing Today
paragraphs: There is no current independent pricing for App Annie, since the brand and product have been fully absorbed first into data.ai and then into Sensor Tower.
Anyone looking for this type of mobile app market intelligence data today needs to evaluate Sensor Tower's current product and pricing directly, rather than App Annie or data.ai as separate options.
Historically, App Annie and data.ai sold enterprise subscription plans with custom, quote-based pricing rather than public self-serve tiers, a pattern that continues under Sensor Tower.

features.description: Historical core offering estimating app performance across iOS and Google Play.

features.description: Tracked how apps ranked over time within app store categories and charts.

features.title: Usage and engagement analytics
features.description: Provided data on how users engaged with apps after download.

features.title: Ad intelligence
features.description: Offered competitive insight into mobile advertising creative and spend.

features.title: Now part of Sensor Tower
features.description: These capabilities have been absorbed into Sensor Tower's combined product line following the 2024 acquisition.

pros: Historically one of the most trusted and widely cited sources of mobile app market data
Long operating history and deep data sets built up over more than a decade
Core capabilities live on, now consolidated within Sensor Tower's broader data panels
cons: App Annie no longer exists as an independent brand or product to purchase
The rebrand and subsequent acquisition history creates confusion for anyone researching the name today
The company's history includes a 2021 SEC securities fraud settlement over data manipulation practices
No current, App Annie-specific pricing or support exists; buyers must go through Sensor Tower instead

faqs.question: Is App Annie still around in 2026?
faqs.answer: Not as an independent brand. It rebranded to data.ai around 2021 to 2022, and data.ai was acquired by Sensor Tower in March 2024. The appannie.com domain now redirects to data.ai, which itself displays a notice about the Sensor Tower acquisition.

faqs.question: What is data.ai?
faqs.answer: data.ai is the rebranded name App Annie adopted in late 2021 to early 2022, before it was acquired by Sensor Tower in 2024.

faqs.question: Who acquired data.ai?
faqs.answer: Sensor Tower acquired data.ai in March 2024 in a deal with undisclosed terms.

faqs.question: Can I still sign up for App Annie or data.ai directly?
faqs.answer: No independent signup exists; the current login page for data.ai displays an acquisition notice directing users to Sensor Tower.

faqs.question: Why did App Annie change its name?
faqs.answer: The company rebranded to data.ai to emphasize an AI-driven data positioning, around the same period it settled SEC charges related to manipulating app performance estimates.

faqs.question: Who founded App Annie?
faqs.answer: Bertrand Schmitt and Yann Lechelle founded App Annie in Beijing in 2010.

faqs.question: What should I use instead of App Annie today?
faqs.answer: Sensor Tower is the direct successor, having absorbed data.ai's technology, data panels, and much of its team.

enrichment.short_description: Appcircle is a mobile DevOps platform that automates CI/CD for iOS, Android, React Native, and Flutter apps, covering build, code signing, testing distribution, and store publishing through a low-code, drag-and-drop workflow builder.
enrichment.headquarters: Istanbul, Turkey (corporate entity registered in Dover, Delaware, USA)
enrichment.seo_title: Appcircle Review 2026: Mobile CI/CD Pricing vs Bitrise
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Appcircle automates mobile app build, testing, and store publishing for iOS and Android. See 2026 pricing, features, and how it compares to Bitrise.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Appcircle is a mobile DevOps platform focused specifically on CI/CD for mobile applications, covering the full pipeline from build and automated code signing through testing distribution to internal testers and final publishing to the Apple App Store and Google Play. The product originated as a spinoff of Smartface Inc, an earlier enterprise mobility company, and Appcircle Inc was established around 2019 to focus specifically on platform-agnostic mobile CI/CD rather than Smartface's more limited scope; the company's engineering and go-to-market operations are centered in Istanbul, Turkey, while the corporate entity is registered in Dover, Delaware, with additional offices referenced in New Jersey and Palo Alto. Appcircle supports native iOS and Android projects as well as cross-platform frameworks including React Native, Flutter, and Xamarin, and it differentiates itself with a drag-and-drop, low-code workflow builder that lets teams assemble build, test, and deployment pipelines visually rather than writing YAML configuration files by hand, which is a notable contrast to code-first competitors like Bitrise and Codemagic. Core modules include Build Workflows for automated compilation across platforms, automated code signing and certificate management (a historically painful part of mobile releases), Testing Distribution for pushing builds to internal or beta testers, an Enterprise App Store for private in-house app distribution outside the public app stores, and one-click Publish workflows to push approved builds directly to the Apple App Store and Google Play. Appcircle also supports both cloud-hosted and self-hosted or on-premises deployment, which appeals to enterprises and regulated industries with stricter data residency or security requirements than typical CI/CD SaaS tools allow. On pricing, Appcircle publishes a free-forever Starter plan capped at 1 concurrent build, a 30-minute maximum build time, 20 builds per month, 1,000 CodePush over-the-air updates per month, 100 testing distribution downloads per month, 100 Enterprise App Store downloads per month, and 5 store publishes per month; beyond that, Appcircle moves to a fully custom Corporate plan priced per module, with selectable machine plans, public/private cloud or on-premises deployment, SSO and LDAP support, multi-tenancy, role-based access management, and dedicated SLA support, meaning there is no published mid-tier self-serve pricing between the free plan and enterprise sales. Appcircle's target audience is mobile development teams, DevOps engineers, and technical leaders at companies building iOS and Android apps who want a unified, low-code CI/CD platform, with particular appeal to enterprises that need on-premises or private cloud deployment options that pure-SaaS competitors like Bitrise, Codemagic, and GitHub Actions with mobile plugins typically don't offer.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is appcircle.io
paragraphs: Appcircle is a mobile DevOps and CI/CD platform purpose-built for iOS and Android app development, automating the build, test, code-signing, and store-publishing steps that mobile teams otherwise have to script and maintain by hand.

The product emerged as a spinoff of Smartface Inc, an earlier enterprise mobility company, with Appcircle established as its own focused platform around 2019. Its engineering and operations are centered in Istanbul, Turkey, with the corporate entity registered in Dover, Delaware, and additional offices referenced in New Jersey and Palo Alto.

Appcircle supports native iOS and Android projects as well as React Native, Flutter, and Xamarin, and is positioned as a low-code alternative to code-first mobile CI/CD tools like Bitrise and Codemagic, with a particular emphasis on offering self-hosted and on-premises deployment for enterprises with stricter compliance needs.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Appcircle's Build Workflows module lets teams define mobile build pipelines through a drag-and-drop interface rather than hand-written YAML, while its automated code signing and certificate management handles one of the most error-prone parts of iOS and Android releases.

Testing Distribution pushes new builds directly to internal or external testers, while the Enterprise App Store module supports private, in-house distribution of apps outside the public Apple App Store and Google Play, useful for internal line-of-business apps.

One-click Publish workflows push approved builds straight to the Apple App Store and Google Play, and the platform supports both fully cloud-hosted deployment and self-hosted or on-premises deployment for enterprises with data residency or security requirements.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Appcircle offers a free-forever Starter plan with 1 concurrent build, a 30-minute maximum build time, 20 builds per month, 1,000 CodePush over-the-air updates per month, 100 testing distribution downloads per month, 100 Enterprise App Store downloads per month, and 5 store publishes per month.
Beyond the free plan, Appcircle moves directly to a custom-quoted Corporate plan, priced per module with selectable machine plans and deployment options (public cloud, private cloud, or on-premises).

The Corporate plan adds SSO and LDAP support, multi-tenancy, role-based access management, and dedicated SLA support, but there is no published fixed-price mid-tier plan; larger teams need to contact sales for a quote.

features.title: Drag-and-drop build workflows
features.description: Visual, low-code pipeline builder for configuring mobile build, test, and deployment steps.

features.title: Automated code signing
features.description: Manages iOS and Android signing certificates and profiles automatically as part of the build process.

features.title: Testing Distribution
features.description: Distributes new builds directly to internal or beta testers for feedback before release.

features.title: Enterprise App Store
features.description: Private, in-house app distribution channel for internal line-of-business apps outside the public app stores.

features.title: One-click store publishing
features.description: Automated publishing workflows that push approved builds to the Apple App Store and Google Play.

features.title: Cross-platform support
features.description: Works with native iOS and Android as well as React Native, Flutter, and Xamarin projects.

features.title: On-premises and private cloud deployment
features.description: Supports self-hosted or private cloud deployment for enterprises with strict compliance or data residency needs.

features.title: CodePush over-the-air updates
features.description: Ship JavaScript and asset updates to React Native apps without a full app store review cycle.

pros: Low-code, drag-and-drop workflow builder lowers the barrier to setting up mobile CI/CD compared to YAML-first tools
Supports on-premises and private cloud deployment, a differentiator versus SaaS-only competitors
Covers the full mobile release lifecycle: build, sign, test-distribute, and publish, in one platform
Free-forever Starter plan is available for small teams or evaluation
Dedicated Enterprise App Store module for private internal app distribution
cons: No published mid-tier pricing; jumping from the free plan to Corporate requires a custom sales conversation
Free plan's build limits (20 builds per month, 30-minute max build time, 1 concurrent build) are restrictive for active teams
Smaller market presence and community compared to Bitrise or Codemagic
Less publicly available information on company funding, size, and detailed track record

faqs.question: What does Appcircle do?
faqs.answer: It automates mobile CI/CD, covering build, automated code signing, testing distribution, and publishing to the Apple App Store and Google Play for iOS, Android, React Native, and Flutter apps.

faqs.question: Is there a free plan?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Starter plan is free forever, with limits including 20 builds per month, 1 concurrent build, and a 30-minute maximum build time.

faqs.question: How much does the paid plan cost?
faqs.answer: Appcircle does not publish fixed mid-tier pricing; the Corporate plan is custom-quoted per module based on usage and deployment needs.

faqs.question: Does Appcircle support on-premises deployment?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Corporate plan supports public cloud, private cloud, or fully on-premises deployment.

faqs.question: How does Appcircle compare to Bitrise or Codemagic?
faqs.answer: Appcircle emphasizes a low-code, drag-and-drop workflow builder and on-premises deployment options, while Bitrise and Codemagic are more code/YAML-first and primarily SaaS-hosted.

faqs.question: What frameworks does Appcircle support?
faqs.answer: Native iOS and Android, plus React Native, Flutter, and Xamarin.

faqs.question: Where is Appcircle based?
faqs.answer: Its engineering and operations are centered in Istanbul, Turkey, with the corporate entity registered in Dover, Delaware, and additional offices referenced in New Jersey and Palo Alto.

faqs.question: What is Appcircle's Enterprise App Store?
faqs.answer: A private distribution channel for internally distributing apps to employees or testers outside the public Apple App Store and Google Play.

use_cases.title: Automating mobile release pipelines
use_cases.description: Replace manual build, signing, and submission steps with an automated, repeatable CI/CD pipeline for iOS and Android apps.

use_cases.title: Internal enterprise app distribution
use_cases.description: Distribute internal line-of-business apps to employees privately, without going through the public app stores.
use_cases.audience: Enterprise IT and internal tooling teams

use_cases.title: Regulated industries needing on-premises CI/CD
use_cases.description: Run mobile CI/CD in a private cloud or fully on-premises environment to meet data residency or security compliance requirements.
use_cases.audience: Enterprises in regulated industries such as finance or healthcare

features: 100 Enterprise App Store downloads per month
Public, private cloud, or on-premises deployment
